full_context: According to IEC 60034-1:2017 "Rotating electrical machines - Part 1: Rating and performance", Chapter 8, motor efficiency is calculated by Formula (8.1): η = P₂/P₁. Where: η is efficiency; P₂ is output power, kW; P₁ is input power, kW. This formula evaluates the energy conversion efficiency of electric machines.

variable_definitions.name: Efficiency
variable_definitions.meaning: Ratio of output power to input power

variable_definitions.name: Output Power
variable_definitions.meaning: Mechanical power output at motor shaft

variable_definitions.name: Input Power
variable_definitions.meaning: Electrical power absorbed from the supply

potential_questions: Given motor input power P₁=100 kW and output power P₂=95 kW, calculate the efficiency η.
task_description: 1. Obtain P₁ and P₂; 2. Calculate η = P₂/P₁; 3. Output η.

full_context: According to IEC 60909:2016 "Short-circuit current calculation in three-phase a.c. systems", Chapter 4, the initial symmetrical short-circuit current is calculated by Formula (4.1): I_k'' = c U_n/(√3 Z_k). Where: I_k'' is initial symmetrical short-circuit current, kA; c is voltage factor; U_n is nominal system voltage, kV; Z_k is short-circuit impedance, Ω. Used for power system fault analysis.

variable_definitions.name: Initial Symmetrical Short-Circuit Current
variable_definitions.meaning: RMS value at the instant of short circuit

variable_definitions.name: Nominal Voltage
variable_definitions.meaning: Rated voltage of the power system

variable_definitions.name: Short-Circuit Impedance
variable_definitions.meaning: Total system impedance seen from fault point

potential_questions: For a 10 kV system: voltage factor c=1.1, U_n=10 kV, Z_k=0.5 Ω. Calculate initial symmetrical short-circuit current I_k''.
task_description: 1. Obtain c, U_n, Z_k; 2. Compute √3 Z_k; 3. Compute c U_n/(√3 Z_k); 4. Output I_k''.

full_context: According to IEC 60287-1-1:2014 "Electric cables - Calculation of the current rating", Chapter 5, cable temperature rise is calculated by Formula (5.1): Δθ = I² R T. Where: Δθ is temperature rise, K; I is load current, A; R is AC resistance per unit length, Ω/m; T is thermal resistance per unit length, K·m/W. Used for cable thermal design.

variable_definitions.name: Temperature Rise
variable_definitions.meaning: Difference between conductor and ambient temperature

variable_definitions.name: Load Current
variable_definitions.meaning: RMS current flowing in the cable

variable_definitions.name: AC Resistance
variable_definitions.meaning: Per-unit-length resistance including skin and proximity effects

potential_questions: Given I=200 A, R=0.0001 Ω/m, T=2.5 K·m/W, calculate temperature rise Δθ.
task_description: 1. Obtain I, R, T; 2. Compute I²; 3. Compute I² × R × T; 4. Output Δθ.

full_context: According to IEEE Std 80-2013 "Guide for Safety in AC Substation Grounding", Chapter 11, grounding resistance of a vertical rod is calculated by Formula (11.1): R_g = ρ/(2πL) ln(4L/d). Where: R_g is grounding resistance, Ω; ρ is soil resistivity, Ω·m; L is rod length, m; d is rod diameter, m. Used for grounding system design.

variable_definitions.name: Grounding Resistance
variable_definitions.meaning: Resistance between grounding electrode and remote earth

variable_definitions.name: Electrode Length
variable_definitions.meaning: Buried length of vertical grounding rod

potential_questions: Given ρ=100 Ω·m, L=3 m, d=0.02 m, calculate grounding resistance R_g.
task_description: 1. Obtain ρ, L, d; 2. Compute 2πL; 3. Compute 4L/d; 4. Compute ln(4L/d); 5. Compute R_g = ρ/(2πL) × ln(4L/d); 6. Output R_g.

full_context: According to IEEE Std 1459-2010 "Definitions for the Measurement of Electric Power Quantities Under Sinusoidal, Nonsinusoidal, Balanced or Unbalanced Conditions", Chapter 3, apparent power is calculated by Formula (3.1): S = √(P² + Q²). Where: S is apparent power, VA; P is active power, W; Q is reactive power, var. Fundamental power triangle relation.

variable_definitions.name: Apparent Power
variable_definitions.meaning: Product of rms voltage and rms current

potential_questions: Given P=800 W, Q=600 var, calculate apparent power S.
task_description: 1. Obtain P and Q; 2. Compute P² + Q²; 3. Compute S = √(P² + Q²); 4. Output S.

full_context: According to ISO 12100:2010 "Safety of machinery - General principles for design - Risk assessment and risk reduction", Appendix B, risk level is estimated by Formula (B.1): R = S × P. Where: R is risk level; S is severity of injury; P is probability of occurrence. Used for machinery safety risk assessment.

variable_definitions.name: Risk Level
variable_definitions.meaning: Combined risk from severity and probability

potential_questions: Given severity S=4 (1–5 scale), probability P=3 (1–5 scale), calculate risk level R.
task_description: 1. Obtain S and P; 2. Compute R = S × P; 3. Output R.

full_context: According to ISO 13857:2019 "Safety of machinery - Safety distances to prevent hazard zones being reached by upper and lower limbs", Chapter 5, safety distance is calculated by Formula (5.1): S = K × T + C. Where: S is safety distance, mm; K is approach speed, mm/s; T is total system stop time, s; C is additional distance, mm. Used for safety guard design.

variable_definitions.name: Safety Distance
variable_definitions.meaning: Minimum distance to prevent access to hazard

variable_definitions.name: Approach Speed
variable_definitions.meaning: Speed of body part toward hazard

variable_definitions.name: Stop Time
variable_definitions.meaning: Time from signal to full stop

variable_definitions.name: Additional Distance
variable_definitions.meaning: Margin for body dimensions and tolerance

potential_questions: Given K=2000 mm/s, T=0.1 s, C=120 mm, calculate safety distance S.
task_description: 1. Obtain K, T, C; 2. Compute K × T; 3. Compute S = K × T + C; 4. Output S.

full_context: According to ISO 14064-1:2018 "Greenhouse gases - Part 1: Specification with guidance at the organization level for quantification and reporting of greenhouse gas emissions and removals", Chapter 6, GHG emissions are calculated by Formula (6.1): E_GHG = Σ (AD_i × EF_i). Where: E_GHG is greenhouse gas emissions, t CO₂e; AD_i is activity data; EF_i is emission factor. Used for carbon footprint calculation.
potential_questions: Given electricity AD₁=1000 MWh, EF₁=0.8 t CO₂e/MWh; gas AD₂=5000 m³, EF₂=2.0 t CO₂e/1000 m³. Calculate total E_GHG.
task_description: 1. Obtain AD_i and EF_i; 2. Compute each AD_i × EF_i; 3. Sum all terms; 4. Output E_GHG.

full_context: According to ISO 14001:2015 "Environmental management systems - Requirements with guidance for use", Appendix A, environmental aspect significance is evaluated by Formula (A.1): E = P × I. Where: E is significance; P is probability; I is impact. Used for environmental aspect evaluation.
potential_questions: Given probability P=0.3, impact I=8 (1–10 scale), calculate significance E.
task_description: 1. Obtain P and I; 2. Compute E = P × I; 3. Output E.

full_context: According to ISO 45001:2018 "Occupational health and safety management systems - Requirements with guidance for use", Chapter 6, occupational health and safety risk is assessed by Formula (6.1): R = L × S. Where: R is risk level; L is likelihood; S is severity. Used for OHS risk assessment.
potential_questions: Given likelihood L=4 (1–5 scale), severity S=5 (1–5 scale), calculate risk R.
task_description: 1. Obtain L and S; 2. Compute R = L × S; 3. Output R.

full_context: According to ISO 50001:2018 "Energy management systems - Requirements with guidance for use", Chapter 6, energy performance indicator is defined by Formula (6.1): EnPI = E/P. Where: EnPI is energy performance indicator; E is energy consumption, tce; P is production output, t. Evaluates energy use efficiency.
potential_questions: Given E=500 tce, P=1000 t, calculate EnPI.
task_description: 1. Obtain E and P; 2. Compute EnPI = E/P; 3. Output EnPI.

full_context: According to ISO 22000:2018 "Food safety management systems - Requirements", Chapter 8, food safety risk is evaluated by Formula (8.1): R = S × O × D. Where: R is risk level; S is severity; O is occurrence; D is detectability. Used for food safety hazard analysis.
potential_questions: Given S=5, O=3, D=2 (all 1–5 scale), calculate risk R.
task_description: 1. Obtain S, O, D; 2. Compute R = S × O × D; 3. Output R.

full_context: According to ISO 13485:2016 "Medical devices - Quality management systems - Requirements for regulatory purposes", Chapter 7, process capability index is calculated by Formula (7.1): C_p = (USL - LSL)/(6σ). Where: C_p is process capability index; USL is upper specification limit; LSL is lower specification limit; σ is process standard deviation. Evaluates production process capability.
potential_questions: Given USL=10.2 mm, LSL=9.8 mm, σ=0.05 mm, calculate C_p.
task_description: 1. Obtain USL, LSL, σ; 2. Compute USL - LSL; 3. Compute 6σ; 4. Compute C_p = (USL - LSL)/(6σ); 5. Output C_p.

full_context: According to ISO 9001:2015 "Quality management systems - Requirements", Chapter 9, process performance is defined by Formula (9.1): P = A/T. Where: P is process performance; A is actual output; T is target output. Used to evaluate process effectiveness.

variable_definitions.name: Process Performance
variable_definitions.meaning: Ratio of actual to target output

potential_questions: Given target T=1000 units, actual A=950 units, calculate performance P.
task_description: 1. Obtain A and T; 2. Compute P = A/T; 3. Output P.

full_context: According to ISO/TS 16949:2016 "Quality management systems - Particular requirements for the application of ISO 9001 for automotive production and relevant service parts organizations", Chapter 8, parts per million defect rate is calculated by Formula (8.1): PPM = (D/N) × 10⁶. Where: PPM is parts per million defects; D is number of defects; N is total inspected quantity. Measures product quality level.
potential_questions: Given N=5000 pcs, D=25 defects, calculate PPM.
task_description: 1. Obtain D and N; 2. Compute D/N; 3. Compute PPM = (D/N) × 10⁶; 4. Output PPM.

full_context: According to AS9100:2016 "Quality management systems - Requirements for aviation, space and defense organizations", Chapter 9, failure rate is calculated by Formula (9.1): FR = F/T. Where: FR is failure rate, 1/h; F is number of failures; T is total operating time, h. Used for reliability evaluation.
potential_questions: Given F=5 failures, T=10000 h, calculate failure rate FR.
task_description: 1. Obtain F and T; 2. Compute FR = F/T; 3. Output FR.

full_context: According to ISO 26262:2018 "Road vehicles - Functional safety", Chapter 9, probability of dangerous failure per hour is calculated by Formula (9.1): PFH = N/T. Where: PFH is dangerous failure rate, 1/h; N is number of dangerous failures; T is total operating time, h. Used for automotive E/E system safety assessment.

variable_definitions.name: Probability of Dangerous Failure per Hour

potential_questions: Given N=2, T=1×10⁶ h, calculate PFH.
task_description: 1. Obtain N and T; 2. Compute PFH = N/T; 3. Output PFH.

full_context: According to IEC 61508:2010 "Functional safety of electrical/electronic/programmable electronic safety-related systems", Chapter 7, average probability of failure on demand is defined by Formula (7.1): PFD_avg = (1/t) ∫₀ᵗ PFD(τ) dτ. Where: PFD_avg is average PFD; t is test interval; PFD(τ) is PFD at time τ. Used for safety instrumented system assessment.

variable_definitions.name: Average Probability of Failure on Demand

potential_questions: Given PFD(t)=λ t, λ=1×10⁻⁵ /h, t=8760 h, calculate PFD_avg.
task_description: 1. Obtain λ and t; 2. Compute ∫₀ᵗ λτ dτ = λ t²/2; 3. Compute PFD_avg = λ t/2; 4. Substitute values; 5. Output PFD_avg.

full_context: According to ISO 14971:2019 "Medical devices - Application of risk management to medical devices", Appendix D, risk acceptability is evaluated by Formula (D.1): R = P × H × D. Where: R is risk; P is probability of harm; H is severity of harm; D is detectability. Used for medical device risk management.
potential_questions: Given P=0.01, H=8 (1–10), D=3 (1–5), calculate risk R.
task_description: 1. Obtain P, H, D; 2. Compute R = P × H × D; 3. Output R.

full_context: According to ISO 31000:2018 "Risk management - Guidelines", Chapter 6, risk level is assessed by Formula (6.1): R = L × C. Where: R is risk level; L is likelihood; C is consequence. Basic risk assessment method.
potential_questions: Given likelihood L=3 (1–5), consequence C=4 (1–5), calculate risk R.
task_description: 1. Obtain L and C; 2. Compute R = L × C; 3. Output R.

full_context: According to ISO 17025:2017 "General requirements for the competence of testing and calibration laboratories", Chapter 7, expanded uncertainty is calculated by Formula (7.1): U = k × u_c. Where: U is expanded uncertainty; k is coverage factor; u_c is combined standard uncertainty. Used for measurement uncertainty evaluation.
potential_questions: Given u_c=0.05 mm, k=2 (~95% confidence), calculate expanded uncertainty U.
task_description: 1. Obtain k and u_c; 2. Compute U = k × u_c; 3. Output U.
